items.q: What is the difference between physical and economic water scarcity?
items.correct: Physical scarcity is insufficient water supply for demand (e.g. arid regions); economic scarcity is inability to access available water due to lack of infrastructure or investment, despite water being present.
items.misconception: Water scarcity only happens in dry countries.
items.random_wrong: Economic scarcity means water is too expensive for governments to buy.
items.why: Sub-Saharan Africa has economic scarcity — there's water but no pipes, pumps or treatment. Students focus on rainfall; the geographical insight is that scarcity is partly an infrastructure/governance issue.

items.correct: The combined loss of water from a surface through evaporation (from soil and water bodies) and transpiration (from plant leaves).
items.misconception: It is only water evaporating from puddles and lakes.
items.random_wrong: It is the process of rain falling from clouds.
items.why: Students focus only on evaporation. Transpiration from forests can move enormous quantities of water — a key concept for understanding deforestation's effect on the water cycle.

items.correct: The sudden release of built-up stress along faults at plate boundaries as rocks slip past each other, generating seismic waves.
items.misconception: Earthquakes are caused by magma rising up to the surface.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes are caused by the Earth's core overheating.
items.why: Students conflate volcanic and seismic processes. Most earthquakes are tectonic (stress release at faults), not volcanic — magma movement is a separate process and most quakes occur at non-volcanic boundaries.
It's 1700 m^3/capita/yr. “700 m^3/capita/yr” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Tony Allan. “Malin Falkenmark” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Falkenmark absolute scarcity threshold in m^3/capita/yr:
items.why: The real figure is 500. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
